interest: Broad public and equity advocates interested in lifting the whole cohort's skills without dismantling the selective track structure, plus governments seeking a politically durable, non-structural reform.
value: Equity vs. excellence, framed as whether raising the floor for the general-school cohort can be achieved without threatening selective-track excellence.
fear: The fear driving it is that structural reform would mobilise middle-class opposition and prove politically unsustainable, so a within-system compensation route feels like the only durable way to avoid leaving disadvantaged pupils behind.
assumption: It assumes the Portuguese TEIP result transfers to Hungary despite Portugal's later selection age (12-15) versus Hungary's 6/8-year entry, i.e. that downstream compensation can offset rather than merely mask a 6-8 year selective-track head-start.
empirical_uncertainty: Contested-to-unknown: the scenario labels the offset claim 'weak' evidence and 'unverifiable ex ante' because no country has offset early-sorting effects at Hungary's selection age, while the TEIP improvement itself rests on 'moderate' evidence (OECD PISA trends, Crato 2020).
